title: Describing people and emotions
description: A2 French: character adjectives with agreement and position, feelings with être and avoir, and expressing opinions. Explained in English.

cards.notes: Most French adjectives agree with the noun they describe. Add **-e** for feminine: *gentil → gentille*, *sérieux → sérieuse*, *paresseux → paresseuse*. English has no agreement, so this is easy to forget.

cards.front: sympathique, timide, sérieux/sérieuse, drôle, paresseux/paresseuse, travailleur/travailleuse
cards.back: friendly, shy, serious, funny, lazy, hard-working
cards.notes: Common character adjectives. Most are placed **after** the noun: *un homme sérieux* (a serious man), *une femme drôle* (a funny woman). Note feminine forms: *sérieux → sérieuse*, *paresseux → paresseuse*, *travailleur → travailleuse*.

A small group of very common adjectives goes **before** the noun: *un bon ami* (a good friend), *une grande ville* (a big city), *un petit chat* (a small cat), *un beau garçon* (a handsome boy). Remember **BAGS**: Beauty, Age, Goodness, Size go before.
cards.hint: BAGS = Beauty, Age, Goodness, Size — these go BEFORE the noun.

cards.notes: Use **être** for emotional or physical states described by adjectives. The adjective agrees with the subject: *je suis content* (m.) / *je suis contente* (f.). Other examples: *heureux/heureuse* (happy), *stressé(e)* (stressed), *inquiet/inquiète* (worried).

cards.front: avoir faim / avoir peur / avoir froid / avoir envie de
cards.back: to be hungry / to be afraid / to be cold / to feel like (doing something)
cards.notes: French uses **avoir** (to have) for many sensations where English uses *to be*. This is a classic trap: **never** say *je suis faim* — it must be *j'ai faim* (I have hunger = I am hungry). Similarly: *j'ai peur* (I am afraid), *j'ai froid* (I am cold), *j'ai envie de dormir* (I feel like sleeping).
cards.hint: English: 'I AM hungry' — French: 'j'AI faim' (I HAVE hunger).

cards.front: je pense que / je trouve que / à mon avis / je crois que
cards.back: I think that / I find that / in my opinion / I believe that
cards.notes: These opinion phrases are all followed by the **indicative** (ordinary) verb form — not the subjunctive at A2. Examples: *Je pense que c'est difficile.* (I think it is difficult.) *À mon avis, il est gentil.* (In my opinion, he is kind.) *Je trouve que le film est drôle.* (I find the film funny.)

cards.front: Il est gentil. / Elle est gentille.
cards.back: He is kind. / She is kind.
cards.notes: *Gentil → gentille*: the feminine doubles the **-l** and adds **-e**. Other doublers: *gros → grosse* (fat/big), *bon → bonne* (good). Pronunciation tip: the final consonant becomes audible in the feminine form.

Adjectives ending in **-eur** often change to **-euse** in the feminine: *travailleur → travailleuse*. Similarly: *menteur → menteuse* (lying/liar). Do not confuse with *travaillant* (the present participle, used more verbally).

cards.back: I am afraid (literally: I have fear)
cards.notes: Remember: **avoir** peur, not *être* peur. You can say what you are afraid of with **de**: *j'ai peur des araignées* (I am afraid of spiders). Other avoir expressions of the same type: *avoir honte* (to be ashamed), *avoir hâte* (to be eager).

A very common feeling adjective. *Je suis content(e) de te voir* — I am happy to see you. Synonym: *heureux/heureuse* (happier in tone). The opposite is *triste* (sad) — which has the same form for both genders.

cards.back: I find that it is funny. / I think it is funny.
cards.notes: *Trouver que* + indicative is a natural, colloquial way to give an opinion. It is slightly softer than *je pense que*. *Drôle* means funny/odd and stays the same for both genders (invariable in this sense).

cards.front: un petit garçon / une grande fille
cards.back: a small boy / a tall girl
cards.notes: *Petit* and *grand* are BAGS adjectives — they go **before** the noun. *Petit → petite* (add -e), *grand → grande* (add -e, final -d is now pronounced). Compare: *une ville grande* (unusual/literary) vs *une grande ville* (normal: a big city).

steps.title: Character adjectives: agreement and position
steps.body:
# Describing people — adjectives

French adjectives must **agree** with the noun they describe (unlike English, which has no agreement). Add **-e** for feminine, **-s** for plural, **-es** for feminine plural.

## Common character adjectives

| Masculine | Feminine | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| gentil | gentille | kind |
| sérieux | sérieuse | serious |
| paresseux | paresseuse | lazy |
| travailleur | travailleuse | hard-working |
| sympathique | sympathique | friendly |
| timide | timide | shy |
| drôle | drôle | funny |

*Sympathique, timide* and *drôle* are invariable for gender (same form for m./f.).

## Position: before or after the noun?

Most adjectives go **after** the noun: *un homme sérieux*, *une femme timide*.

But the **BAGS** group (Beauty, Age, Goodness, Size) goes **before**:

| Adjective | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| beau/belle | beautiful | un beau jour |
| jeune | young | une jeune femme |
| bon/bonne | good | un bon ami |
| grand/grande | big/tall | une grande maison |
| petit/petite | small | un petit chien |

> **English-speaker tip:** English adjectives always go before the noun. In French, the default is *after*. Only memorise the short list that goes before.

steps.title: Expressing feelings: être vs avoir
steps.body:
## Feelings with être

Use **être** + adjective for emotional or physical states:

- *Je suis content(e).* — I am happy.
- *Il est triste.* — He is sad.
- *Nous sommes fatigués.* — We are tired.

The adjective agrees with the subject (content/contente, fatigué/fatiguée).

## Feelings with avoir — the English trap

For **physical sensations**, French uses **avoir** (to have) where English uses *to be*:

| French | Literal | English |
|---|---|---|
| j'ai faim | I have hunger | I am hungry |
| j'ai soif | I have thirst | I am thirsty |
| j'ai peur | I have fear | I am afraid |
| j'ai froid | I have cold | I am cold |
| j'ai chaud | I have heat | I am hot |
| j'ai envie de + inf. | I have desire to | I feel like -ing |

> **Never say** *je suis faim* or *je suis peur* — these are ungrammatical. The verb must be **avoir**.

steps.title: Giving opinions
steps.body:
## Opinion phrases

At A2, use these four phrases to express opinions. All are followed by **que** + a normal (indicative) verb:

| Phrase | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Je pense que** | I think that | *Je pense que c'est vrai.* |
| **Je trouve que** | I find/think that | *Je trouve que c'est drôle.* |
| **Je crois que** | I believe that | *Je crois qu'il est gentil.* |
| **À mon avis** | In my opinion | *À mon avis, elle est sérieuse.* |

*À mon avis* does **not** use *que* — it stands alone at the start: *À mon avis, le film est long.*

## Putting it all together

Combine an opinion phrase + adjective in context:

- *Je pense qu'il est très **travailleur**.* — I think he is very hard-working.
- *À mon avis, elle n'est pas **timide**.* — In my opinion, she is not shy.
- *Je trouve que tu es **drôle**!* — I think you are funny!

steps.title: Match the adjective to its meaning

exercise.prompt: Match each French adjective to its English meaning.
Translate: *She is kind.* (use gentil — remember the feminine form!)
exercise.hint: gentil (m.) → gentille (f.): double the -l and add -e.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I am hungry.* (careful — English says 'am', French does NOT use être here!)
exercise.hint: French uses avoir for hunger: j'ai faim (I have hunger).

exercise.prompt: Fill in the blank with the correct opinion phrase (in my opinion).
exercise.hint: The phrase meaning 'in my opinion' does not use que.

steps.title: Build: He is a serious man

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words to say: *He is a serious man.* (adjective after the noun!)
exercise.hint: sérieux goes AFTER homme — most adjectives follow the noun.

steps.title: Which sentence uses the adjective correctly?

exercise.prompt: Which sentence correctly describes a small house?
exercise.hint: petit is a BAGS adjective — it goes before the noun. Also check gender: maison is feminine.

steps.title: Match the avoir expression to its English meaning

exercise.prompt: Match each avoir expression to its English equivalent.
Translate: *I think that he is funny.*
exercise.hint: Je pense que / je trouve que + indicative. Use que → qu' before a vowel.
distractors: Je pense que il est drôle.
À mon avis que il est drôle.

steps.title: Fill in the correct adjective form

exercise.prompt: Complete the sentence with the feminine form of travailleur.

steps.title: Build: I am afraid of the dog

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *I am afraid of the dog.*
exercise.hint: Use avoir (j'ai) — never être — for avoir peur.
